Article
A Brief History of Ergonomic Seating and the 90° Problem
How the office chair became the standard, why the 90° norm was never questioned, and the long line of innovators who tried to solve it.
5 min read
Read more
The 90° chair was never designed. It was inherited.

The office chair as we know it didn't emerge from scientific research into how bodies work. It emerged from the industrial revolution's relationship with right angles, rectangular desks, rectangular rooms, and rectangular manufacturing processes. When the modern office chair took shape in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, 90° was simply what furniture looked like. No ergonomist signed off on it. No anatomist validated it. It was a manufacturing convention that hardened, over decades, into an unquestioned standard.

Work on chair comfort began in earnest in the 1940s and 1950s, around the same time as the term "ergonomics" was formally introduced, derived from the Greek ergon (work) and nomos (natural laws). But by then the 90° posture was so entrenched that researchers didn't question the angle itself. They asked instead: how do we make sitting at 90° less painful? This framing error has persisted ever since.

1973 — NASA sees something the furniture industry missed

The first serious challenge to the 90° assumption didn't come from a chair designer. It came from outer space. When astronauts aboard Skylab, NASA's first space station, were photographed in microgravity while relaxed, their bodies didn't default to 90°. They opened up, knees flexed, hips open, spine decompressed. NASA documented this as "neutral body posture": the position the human body naturally assumes when gravity stops pulling on it. They subsequently published specifications based on these observations, research that has since informed seat design in everything from aircraft cockpits to car seats.

The implication was profound, and largely ignored by the furniture industry: if the body's natural resting state involves an open hip angle, then 90° sitting isn't neutral — it's a forced deviation from what the body actually wants.

1976 — Mandal names the problem

Danish surgeon A.C. Mandal was the first researcher to translate this principle into a systematic challenge to the office chair. His work established what the NASA photographs implied: a forward-sloping seat tips the pelvis forward, opens the angle between torso and thigh, and allows the spine to find its natural alignment. His research, conducted from the 1960s through the 1990s, consistently showed that forward seat tilt was a more suitable position for long periods of sitting than the 90° standard. His preferred model was a "half-standing" posture, a hip angle approaching 135°. The research was rigorous. The conclusions were clear. The industry largely ignored him.

1979 — The Scandinavians try to build it

Mandal's work did reach a small group of Norwegian designers who took it seriously. In 1976, Hans Christian Mengshoel began investigating how people could sit in more balanced ways. His research confirmed the forward-tilt principle, but immediately surfaced the central obstacle: when a seat tilts forward, users slide off. His solution was shin support — a kneeling pad to anchor the body while maintaining the open hip angle.

Working with designer Peter Opsvik, he brought the concept to market as the Balans chair, first exhibited at the 1979 Scandinavian Furniture Fair in Copenhagen. It never achieved mainstream adoption. The kneeling position was uncomfortable for extended use, provided no back support, and was socially incongruous in most office environments. Imitators produced cheaper versions without Opsvik's design rigour, generating complaints that further damaged the category's reputation.

The unsolved problem

Every forward-tilt design that followed faced the same obstacle. Saddle chairs preserved the open hip angle but offered no back support and shared the sliding problem. Modified conventional chairs with forward-tilt adjustment didn't tilt far enough, and users reported feeling like they were sliding out. The correct angle was known. The mechanism to hold a person in it comfortably, with back support, without sliding, did not exist.

For fifty years, everyone who understood the problem had the same solution — and the same unsolvable obstacle.

Until the pivot point changed.

Article
Klaus Kummer — From Mandal to Levitask
The 40-year personal journey of the inventor of the T-Mech™ mechanism — from early research to the founding of Levitask.
5 min read
Read more

When Klaus Kummer encountered Mandal's research in the early 1980s, his reaction was not scepticism — it was recognition. The Danish surgeon had identified the correct principle: a forward-sloping seat opens the hip angle, tips the pelvis into its natural anterior tilt, and restores the spine's alignment. The research was solid. The anatomy was right. The problem was purely mechanical — and every attempt to build a chair on this principle had failed because the user slid off the seat. For Klaus, this was not a reason to abandon the principle. It was a problem to solve.

The question nobody had asked

Every forward-tilt design before Klaus shared a common assumption: the seat tilts around an axis located somewhere under the seat, typically at its centre or rear. This seems logical. It's also, Klaus eventually concluded, why they all failed.

When a seat tilts forward around a central or rear axis, the front of the seat drops and the rear rises. The user's body follows the slope forward under gravity. There is no structural reason for the thighs to stay in contact with the seat. Friction surfaces, contoured seat pans, shin pads, and body harnesses were all attempts to compensate for a geometry that was working against the user from the start.

Klaus asked a different question: what if the axis of rotation wasn't under the seat at all?

Four decades, one pivot point

Working alongside a career as an industrial designer and ergonomics consultant, Klaus spent decades prototyping and testing a different approach. The insight that eventually became the T-Mech™ mechanism was this: if the seat tilts around an axis positioned at the user's knee rather than beneath the seat, the geometry changes entirely. As the seat tilts forward, the front drops — but the thigh, anchored by the knee pivot point, maintains contact with the seat surface. The user's own skeletal structure prevents sliding. No friction required. No shin pads. No harness.

The principle was elegant. Making it manufacturable, durable, and comfortable across the full range of human body sizes was the work of forty years.

The meeting that became Levitask

By the time Klaus had a working prototype of the T-Mech™ knee pivot arc, he had solved the problem he had set out to solve in the 1980s. What he didn't have was a path to market. Translating a mechanism that worked in a workshop into a product that could be manufactured at scale, priced for a premium market, and sold globally required a different kind of expertise entirely.

Klaus and Michael Gotfrid met through a mutual connection in the design world. Michael recognised immediately that what Klaus had built was not an incremental improvement on existing ergonomic chairs — it was the solution to a problem that had resisted the entire industry for fifty years. The commercial opportunity was not "a better chair." It was a new category.

Form follows physics

Levitask was founded on a straightforward premise: build a product worthy of the T-Mech™ mechanism. Not the cheapest possible housing for a good mechanism, but a chair designed from first principles — materials chosen for longevity, components designed to be user-replaceable, aesthetics that emerged honestly from the engineering geometry. The Vision Chair's distinctive silhouette wasn't styled after the mechanism was finished. The form is a consequence of the physics. It couldn't look any other way. This is what happens when forty years of German engineering rigour meets a Scandinavian design tradition — from Mandal to Opsvik — that always believed form and function are the same thing.

What this means for you as a distributor: The story of Klaus Kummer matters commercially, not just as a founding narrative. It answers the question every serious prospect will eventually ask: why can't a competitor simply copy this?

The T-Mech™ knee pivot arc is patent-pending on the mechanical principle, not just on a particular form. And behind the patent is forty years of prototyping, failure, and refinement that cannot be compressed. A competitor can observe the Vision Chair. They cannot shortcut the process that produced it.

Article
What is Elevated Seating?
The core concept behind Levitask. How a 115–120° hip angle changes everything about how we sit.
4 min read
Read more

Elevated Seating is not a feature. It is a different premise entirely.

When Klaus solved the sliding problem — the mechanical obstacle that had blocked every forward-tilt design since Mandal's research in the 1970s — something became possible that hadn't been before: a chair that could deliver the correct hip angle, with full back support, in comfort, for an entire working day. The principle had been understood for decades. It finally had a mechanism worthy of it.

Levitask named that principle Elevated Seating. It is not a generic description of forward-tilt design. It is Levitask's proprietary framing for a specific, measurable postural outcome: opening the hip angle to 115–120°. The Vision Chair is its only commercial embodiment. Elevated Seating is not a feature of the chair — it is the new category the Vision Chair creates.

Understanding Elevated Seating is the foundation of everything else in this portal. It is the answer to the first question any prospect will ask: what makes this different from every other chair on the market? The Vision Chair bridges the gap between seating and standing, delivering the postural benefits of both, without the compromises of either.

The principle in four points
The angle
115–120° hip angle vs. the industry-standard 90°
The mechanism
Knee pivot arc — prevents sliding without friction or harness
The result
Pelvis rotates forward naturally. Natural S-curve restored. No lumbar support needed.
The benefit
Less pain, deeper focus, improved productivity.
Spine Render GIF — asset coming from Veronika

The one thing to remember: At 90°, the body fights its chair. At 115°, it doesn't have to. Every other detail — the health benefits, the research behind it, the product itself — flows from this single shift in angle.

Video
T-Mech™ Technology — The Mechanism Explained
Klaus Kummer explains the patent-pending knee pivot arc and why it solves what 50 years of forward-tilt design could not.

Klaus Kummer — The T-Mech™ mechanism explained
Coming soon

The T-Mech™ (Tilting Mechanism) is the patent-pending system at the heart of the Vision Chair. Unlike every previous forward-tilt design, T-Mech™ uses a knee pivot arc: the axis of rotation is positioned at the user's knee, not at the seat centre. This means that as the seat tilts forward, the thigh remains in contact with the seat surface. The geometry of the user's own skeleton prevents sliding.

Article
Features & Adjustment System
A complete walkthrough of all adjustment points and features on the Vision Chair — what each does and why it matters.
5 min read
Read more

Every adjustment on the Vision Chair is designed to reinforce the Elevated Seating principle — never compromise it. The goal is always to adapt the chair to the user's body, not to force the user into a fixed position.

The Seat
Anti Slip Matrix™

The seat surface combines a micro-dot texture with a contour cradle cavity — two features that work together to keep the user in position when the T-Mech™ elevation is engaged. This is the physical answer to the sliding problem that defeated every forward-tilt design before the Vision Chair.

Adjustments
Seat Height
Right-side lever

358–465 mm seat height range. First adjustment to make — set so feet are flat on the floor and knees are below hip level.

Seat Elevation — T-Mech™
Left-side lever

-5° to +18° forward tilt. This is the core adjustment — it activates Elevated Seating.

Slightly rise off the seat before engaging the lever. The mechanism requires reduced weight on the seat to activate smoothly.
Seat Depth & Backrest Height
Two one-time adjustments. Set once, forget.
Seat Depth
Knob — underside rear of seat

Rotate to slide the seat pan forward or backward. Aim for a 2–3 finger gap between the seat edge and the back of your knees.

Backrest Height
Knob — aluminium bar on backrest

Rotate to align the backrest with the natural curve of your lower back.

Backrest Recline
Automatic — no lever or dial

The backrest follows the user's body movement dynamically — it does not lock into a fixed angle. Lean back and it follows. Sit forward and it returns.

Features
Both models
Folding Armrest

Folds flat when not needed. Ideal for users who prefer an unobstructed workspace.

Both models
4D Adjustable Armrest

Height, depth, and horizontal position adjustable. Shoulders should be relaxed, not raised.

Vision X only
2-Axis Headrest

Adjustable in height and depth. Designed for reclined postures and video calls.

Unlike conventional chairs, the Vision Chair has no "default" position. Explore each adjustment and let your body tell you where it wants to be.

Science
3 items
Article
Spine Biomechanics — Why 90° Fails, Why 115° Works
The anatomical and mechanical explanation for what happens to the spine, pelvis, and muscles at each sitting angle.
6 min read
Read more
90° Sitting
What happens at 90°

When you sit at 90°, your hip flexors — the muscles connecting your femur to your pelvis — are compressed to their minimum length. This triggers an involuntary mechanical chain reaction that no amount of postural awareness can override.

Pelvis rotates posteriorly — base tips forward, top tilts backward
Lumbar spine loses its natural inward curve — the lordosis flattens
Thoracic spine rounds forward, cervical spine follows
Head moves in front of the body's centre of gravity

The result: the L4–L5 and L5–S1 intervertebral discs — the most load-bearing in the lumbar region — absorb significant compressive forces. The surrounding musculature contracts continuously to hold this position. Within minutes, fatigue sets in. Within hours, pain follows.

This is not a question of discipline or posture awareness. It is physics. The body is being held in a position it was not designed to sustain.

115° Sitting
What changes at 115°

At 115°, the mechanical chain reaction reverses — and it does so without any conscious effort from the user.

Hip flexors reach their natural resting length and release
Pelvis rotates anteriorly — lumbar lordosis restores automatically
Thoracic spine decompresses, cervical spine realigns
Head returns above the body's centre of gravity

Compressive load on the L4–L5 and L5–S1 discs drops significantly — approaching the levels measured in standing posture. The deep stabilising muscles of the trunk engage passively, without the compensatory contractions that cause fatigue at 90°. Breathing deepens as the diaphragm, no longer compressed by a forward-flexed thorax, can expand fully.

The body is no longer fighting its chair. It is simply sitting — the way it was designed to.

Infographic
6 Health Benefits of Elevated Seating
The physiological effects of the 115° hip angle — from spinal decompression to improved cognitive performance.
3 min
View
Balanced Pressure Distribution
Even weight distribution reduces pressure hotspots across the spine and lower body.
→ No more back pain at the end of the day
Spinal Alignment
Supports the spine's natural curvature, minimizing postural stress.
→ Sit for hours without the urge to fidget or get up
Optimal Pelvic Rotation
Anterior tilt realigns the pelvis, anchoring the lumbar spine in its natural "S" shape.
→ Better posture without thinking about it
Open Knee Angle
Expands lower body angles, improving circulation and reducing tension in the hips and knees.
→ No more heavy legs after a long day at your desk
Decompressed Lungs
Upright posture reduces thoracic compression, enhancing lung expansion.
→ Deeper breathing — more energy through the day
Pressure-Free Organs
Elevated angle minimizes compression of abdominal organs for better internal balance.
→ Less digestive discomfort during long working sessions
Article
The Research Behind Elevated Seating
The scientific foundations — key studies and references that validate the Elevated Seating principle.
5 min read
Read more

The science behind Elevated Seating draws on decades of independent research in spinal biomechanics, ergonomics, and posture physiology. The following studies form the core of the evidence base that Levitask's claims are built upon. All citations are subject to validation by Klaus Kummer.

1953
Alterations of the lumbar curve related to posture and seating
Lumbar curve
Keegan, J.J. — Journal of Bone and Joint Surgery, 1953
One of the earliest radiological studies on seated posture. Through X-ray analysis, Keegan found that the optimal angle between the trunk and thighs for maintaining a healthy lumbar curve is approximately 135°. His work established the anatomical baseline that later researchers including Mandal built upon — and directly prefigures the 115–120° principle behind Elevated Seating.
Read the study
1981
Disc pressure measurements
Disc pressure
Nachemson, A.L. — Spine, 1981
Nachemson's consolidation of over 20 years of in vivo intradiscal pressure measurements at L3–L4 and L4–L5 across different body positions. Established that sitting generates higher spinal loading than standing, and that posture variation significantly influences disc pressure. A foundational reference for seated posture research.
Read the study
1981
The seated man (Homo Sedens) — the seated work position
Forward tilt
Mandal, A.C. — Applied Ergonomics, 1981
Mandal's comprehensive work establishing that a forward seat tilt of approximately 20° significantly increases the base angle of the spine and ensures lumbar lordosis. He argued the seated work position should approach a half-standing posture with a hip angle of 120–135°. Also observed that pressure on the front third of the seat was nearly twice as high with conventional seating compared to forward-tilt seating — with direct implications for leg circulation. The theoretical foundation for Elevated Seating.
Read the study
1985
A preliminary study of chairs with forward-sloping seats and sitting postures
Spinal curvature
Bridger, R.S. — South African Journal of Physiotherapy, 1985
Bridger's early controlled study identifying that forward-tilt seating aids in maintaining lumbar lordosis, reducing the flattening of the lumbar spine commonly associated with traditional flat or rear-sloping seats. One of the first studies to provide direct evidence of the benefits of forward-tilt seating on spinal alignment. Bridger later expanded this work in a 1989 publication in Human Factors measuring lumbar and thoracic spinal angles across four sitting postures.
Read the 1989 follow-up study
2011
Effect of different seat slopes on lower back muscle activity and fatigue
Muscle fatigue
Park, J. & Yoon, H. — 2011
This study found that forward-tilt seating significantly reduced muscle fatigue in the lower back and improved overall muscle endurance during prolonged sitting tasks. A key finding supporting the practical case that Elevated Seating reduces the end-of-day fatigue experienced by office workers.
2020
Comparison of cervical muscle activity and spinal curvatures in different sitting positions
Muscle activity
Yim, J., Park, J., Lohman, E. & Do, K. — Medicine, 2020
Compared cervical erector spinae (CES) muscle activity across forward-tilt, flat, and rear-sloping seats. Found that forward-tilt seating significantly reduced CES activity (2.67% vs 5.45% for flat and 6.77% for rear-sloping), indicating measurably less strain on the neck and upper back muscles — directly supporting the fatigue reduction benefit of Elevated Seating.
Read the study

Article
The Seating Landscape — Where Levitask Fits
An honest map of the competitive environment: what each category offers, where it falls short, and why Elevated Seating is a distinct category.
5 min read
Read more

The seated furniture market can be divided into five broad approaches to the problem of back pain and postural fatigue. Understanding each helps distributors explain why the Vision Chair is not competing within an existing category — it is creating a new one.

CategoryCore PremiseCore Limitation
Traditional ergonomic chairs Better lumbar support at 90° Treats the symptom, not the root cause. Trapped in the 90° paradigm. Users inevitably hunch.
Saddle chairs Forward tilt, open hip angle No back support, unable to recline. Uncomfortable for extended use. Users slide forward. Not suitable for most office environments.
Balance stools / balls Core activation through instability Unsafe, structurally unsupported, fatigue-inducing. Not accepted in professional environments.
Kneeling chairs Forward tilt + knee stoppers to prevent sliding Knee pressure over time, no backrest, socially incongruous in professional environments.
Gaming chairs Premium aesthetics, heavy padding, lumbar pillows Compensate bad posture with superficial comfort. Ignore the core problem entirely.
Vision Chair (Elevated Seating) Correct hip angle + full back support + comfort New category requiring education.

The positioning principle: Don't position the Vision Chair as another ergonomic chair. Position it as a different category entirely. "Traditional ergonomic chairs are sophisticated solutions to the wrong problem. They optimize 90° sitting, we eliminate it. That's not competition. That's a different category."

Article
The Market Opportunity — Why Now
The macro forces creating the right conditions for a seated posture revolution: remote work, corporate wellness, and the economics of back pain.
4 min read
Read more
#1
Cause of disability worldwide — back pain (WHO)
70%
Of waking hours spent sitting
8–10h
Per day at desk work
80%
Of office workers experience back pain
Six converging market trends
01
Health & Wellness Focus
HR departments are now measured on employee wellbeing metrics — and musculoskeletal disorders are the #1 cause of workplace absenteeism in most developed markets. Ergonomics has moved from a facilities decision to a strategic HR investment. The Vision Chair is a measurable, defensible wellness expenditure.
→ Preventive health > reactive treatment
02
Standing Desk Adoption
The standing desk movement has educated millions of workers on the dangers of static sitting — but standing all day isn't sustainable either. Companies that invested in standing desks are now looking for the next step. Elevated Seating is the natural complement: the posture benefit of standing, delivered while seated.
→ Elevated seating = natural complement to standing desks
03
ESG & Employee Wellbeing Reporting
Companies are increasingly required to report on employee wellbeing as part of their ESG commitments. Investing in ergonomic infrastructure is no longer just good practice — it's a reportable, auditable signal of responsible employer behaviour. The Vision Chair is a documented investment in workforce health.
→ Ergonomics = measurable ESG commitment
04
War for Talent & Employee Retention
In competitive talent markets, the quality of the work environment is a retention signal. Premium ergonomic furniture communicates that an employer takes employee health seriously — and top candidates notice. The Vision Chair is a visible, tangible commitment to the people who sit in it every day.
→ Premium workspace = talent attraction & retention signal
05
Sustainability Movement
Procurement teams are under increasing pressure to justify long-term value over short-term cost. A chair backed by a 10-year warranty, built from premium materials, with user-replaceable components is a fundamentally different financial proposition than furniture replaced every 4–5 years. One purchase, one decade of use.
→ Buy-it-for-life = lower total cost of ownership
06
Remote Work Revolution
Home office upgrades have become a priority for millions of workers who now own their workspace for the first time. The home office furniture market is a high-intent B2C channel that barely existed at scale before 2020.
→ Home office upgrades = priority

The timing argument: The technology behind Elevated Seating has existed for years — but the market conditions to commercialise it at scale have only recently converged. This is the window.
